title: How the gut microbiome relates to neuroinflammatory diseases

chunks.title: Gut Dysbiosis and Western Diets
chunks.summary: The discussion starts with the role of undigested food in the gut microbiome and its association with neuroinflammatory diseases. The speaker emphasizes the detrimental effects of western diets, particularly gut dysbiosis, intestinal inflammation, and leaky gut, which lead to chronic systemic inflammation.
chunks.transcript: so this next section of the podcast will be me trying to connect the dots no one really knows the answer here but these are some of my ideas and some of the literature that i think might support those hypotheses and or ideas so this is a very interesting study looking at the role of undigested food in the gut microbiome possibly cooperating in the pathogenesis of neuroinflammatory diseases a matter of barriers and a proposal on the origin of oregon specificity this is an interesting paper from october of 2019 they say gut dysbiosis which is a fancy word for the overgrowth of the wrong type of bacteria and i'll read it again gut dysbiosis as a consequence of western diets which is an interesting fraud statement leads to intestinal inflammation and a leaky intestinal barrier i think that's a pretty reasonable statement as i talked about in my book the carnivore code no one really knows what aspect of those western diets lead to this but it's pretty clear that a western diet will lead to intestinal inflammation and a leaky intestinal barrier and got dysbiosis in many individuals but life is about having fun so you should just drink a beer and eat your kale and not ask questions this is why this pisses me off so much guys there are people suffering we know that western diets are a problem let's ask questions and understand what it is about these diets that is causing issues for people so that people may stop  suffering gut dysbiosis as a consequence of western diets lead to intestinal inflammation and a leaky intestinal

chunks.title: Neuroinflammation and Gut Health
chunks.summary: The speaker presents a hypothesis linking chronic neuroinflammation to gut health and dysbiosis. He discusses how the opening of the blood-brain barrier may lead to microglia activation and the potential impact of systemic inflammation on neurological conditions.
chunks.transcript: barrier the e-flux which is the movement of undigested food microbes and the toxins like lipoproteins um [Music] the the efflux of undigested food microbes endotoxins as well as immune competent cells and molecules causes chronic systemic inflammation so what they're talking about is the movement of all of these things either across the gut lining because you know that very adjacent to the endothelium of the gut the inner layer of your gut that separates tons and tons of poop and bacteria and undries undigested food from your immune system which live in the layman appropriate which is a level which is a layer of your immune system a layer of cells that is wrapped around the gut okay the movement of unadjusted food microbes and endotoxins as well as immune components cells and molecules between those two places causes chronic systemic inflammation i think that's a very reasonable hypothesis i think we see that being played out and i think that the gut is ground zero for much of it much of this and i think that the gut is ground zero for much of this they go on to say opening of the blood-brain barrier may trigger microglia those are those these are the brain-derived macrophages the brain-derived immune cells it may trigger those cells in astrocytes and set up neuroinflammation i think this is a very reasonable hypothesis and so we are back to the gut and i think the gut is the main place the

chunks.title: Psychiatric Illnesses and Eating Disorders
chunks.summary: A review of literature highlights the connection between gut microbiome health and psychiatric illnesses, especially eating disorders. The speaker discusses how dysregulation in eating behaviors can be linked to alterations in the gut microbiota and immune responses.
chunks.transcript: neurodegenerative diseases are associated with low-grade chronic inflammation we know this it's all quite clear the question is where is it coming from as i say here i think it's quite reasonable that could be coming from dysbiosis the gut etc and then the question becomes how do we do that how do we affect that negatively how do we affect it positively we'll get to my ideas about that in just one moment one more paper the role of the gut microbiome immunity and neuroinflammation in the pathology in the pathophysiology of eating disorders so this is back to eating disorders they say here there's a growing recognition that both the gut microbiome and the immune system are involved in a number of psychiatric illnesses including eating disorders yes i would agree with that statement should come as no surprise given the important roles of diet composition eating patterns and daily caloric intake in modulating both biological systems here we review the evidence that alterations in the gut microbiome and immune system may serve not only to maintain and exacerbate dysregulated eating behavior characterized by caloric restriction and anorexia in anorexia nervosa or binge eating and bulimia nervosa and eating disorder but may also serve as biomarkers of increased risk for developing an eating disorder i think this is a great little review if you want to dig into this and you can see that they say similar to anorexia nervosa highly effective treatments for bulimic syndromes including bulimia and binge eating disorder are lacking because we don't really have a good understanding of how to characterize and then perhaps reverse this neural inflammation or where it's coming from they go on to say the last few years have however led to a growing recognition that both the gut microbiome and the immune system are involved in a number of psychiatric illnesses including eating disorders

chunks.title: Inflammation in Eating Disorders
chunks.summary: The discussion delves into the specific immune changes associated with eating disorders like anorexia and bulimia. Evidence is presented showing significant inflammation and dysbiosis in patients with these conditions, underscoring the critical need for understanding the underlying mechanisms.
chunks.transcript: you can see from this table in the same paper that there's an examination of the gut microbiome and anorexia nervosa and animal models of anorexia nerosa multiple papers which show changes in the microbiome um in anorexia universal decreased alpha diversity increase increased closeritial species increased enterobacteriaceae et cetera et cetera degrees of diversity these are all essentially characteristic of dysbiosis so we can talk about how all this happens in a moment uh they go on they show many of these are changing um with those conditions and then they have other studies later on in this paper where they suggest summary of peripheral and central immune changes in anorexia nebrosa and animal models of anorexia nervosa there are multiple papers decreased neutrophil chemotaxis reduction and granulocyte ability to kill bacteria increased flow inflammatory cytokines il-6 the il-1 beta and tnf alpha like we saw in depression increased increased pro-inflammatory cytokine tnf alpha il-15 v cam-1 il-6 dysregulated t-cell subtypes decreased levels of igm and igg antibodies increased pro-inflammatory carcinoids so there's a lot of inflammation here there's a lot of evidence that there is inflammation neuroinflammation in patients with these eating disorders but they don't really get treated that way we don't really ask these questions too often the same is seen in bulimia nervosa branch eating disorders and animal models of those diseases so i think that what we're starting to see here is a really interesting rabbit hole to fall down
